Uniform Flow Around a Circle

Consider a uniform flow at speed @@math:20:4c590f@@ in the @@math:21:8d048b@@-direction, with complex
                    potential @@math:22:cfa708@@ If we insert an impermeable circular obstacle, at @@math:23:1f937f@@ say,
                    then the flow will be disturbed as illustrated in the image below.
                    The challenge here is to calculate the disturbed flow.

Here we can use a well known result in fluid
                    dynamics established by the English mathematician
                    [[a href="http://mathshistory.st-andrews.ac.uk/Biographies/Milne-Thomson.html" target="_blank"]]L. M.
                        Milne-Thompson[[/a]]:

Now we are interested to know what happens on the circular boundary @@math:41:7d4acc@@

                    Thus

                    which is entirely real. Therefore on the boundary @@math:43:4ca9bf@@
                    @@math:16:b9551b@@
                    This shows that the circle @@math:44:a78ad8@@ is a streamline.

Notice that the complex potential @@math:45:7b2ffb@@ satisfies the hypothesis of the Circle Theorem. We
                    can therefore obtain the complex potential of the uniform flow around a circle
                    by substituting @@math:46:339f4c@@ into equation (\ref{circle-theorem}):
                    @@math:8:d927d1@@

Consequently, the stream function is just the imaginary part of (\ref{potential}), namely
                    @@math:9:f12e0d@@
                    and we can see that the circle @@math:47:6b7e1a@@ is indeed a streamline, with @@math:48:15af3e@@ The resulting

Uniform flow around a circle (right).

You probably have noticed that (\ref{potential}) has a singularity at @@math:50:b59de5@@ This
                    kind of singularity is known as a [[em]]doublet[[/em]] and corresponds to the
                    function @@math:51:e28985@@ The singularity at the origin is inside the obstacle and
                    thus does not affect the external flow. The full streamline pattern, including
                    the doublet inside the circle, is shown in Figure 2.
Streamlines caused by a doublet in a uniform flow.

Uniform flow around circle with circulation

If we add a vortex to the complex potential defined in (\ref{potential}), we obtain
                    a uniform flow around a circle with circulation:
                    @@math:11:13f2c9@@
                    where @@math:55:0668a7@@ represents the [[em]]circulation[[/em]] about the circle.

In this case, the streamfunction is
@@math:12:5bd8e3@@
Notice that the circle @@math:56:aa32d2@@ is still a streamline, with @@math:57:d92909@@ Any stagnation point in the flow satisfies the equation @@math:17:0fd1cb@@ which can be rearranged to the quadratic equation @@math:18:79c7ff@@ The roots of this equation are @@math:19:1335ea@@

Thus when @@math:58:eb00cb@@ there is no circulation with
                    stagnation points at @@math:59:308e0a@@ As @@math:60:7fc634@@ increases, the anticlockwise
                    circulation causes the stagnation points to move upwards around the circle.
                    When it reaches the value 1, the two stagnation points coalesce at the top of the
                    cylinder @@math:61:cdc717@@ If @@math:62:95aa5b@@ then one stagnation point moves into the
                    flow; the other one is inside the circle.

Explore all the cases in the applet below which shows the flow and a circle of
                    radius 1. Drag the sliders @@math:63:03086f@@ and @@math:64:aac86d@@ to change speed and circulation, respectively.

where @@math:66:31e5ff@@ is the speed, @@math:67:4b01e6@@ is the radius and @@math:68:a94676@@ is the circulation.

Uniform Flow Around a Circle

Consider a uniform flow at speed @@math:20:4c590f@@ in the @@math:21:8d048b@@-direction, with complex potential @@math:22:cfa708@@ If we insert an impermeable circular obstacle, at @@math:23:1f937f@@ say, then the flow will be disturbed as illustrated in the image below. The challenge here is to calculate the disturbed flow.

@@verbatim:4:c62732@@

Here we can use a well known result in fluid dynamics established by the English mathematician L. M. Milne-Thompson:

Suppose a complex potential @@math:24:d870fb@@ is given such that any singularities in @@math:25:ed7deb@@ occur in @@math:26:82ce82@@ Then the potential @@math:6:2c2fc5@@ (where the bar denotes complex conjugate) has the same singularities as @@math:27:0f5945@@ in @@math:28:b741c6@@ and the circle @@math:29:a90c17@@ is a streamline.
Let @@math:30:6bd686@@ be a complex potential such that any singularities occur only in the region @@math:31:5e6b77@@ (with @@math:32:4145cb@@) and define @@math:7:8fad19@@ Notice that if @@math:33:afa151@@ then @@math:34:069559@@ Thus, since @@math:35:fb8ffc@@ has no singularities in @@math:36:98c6d7@@ it follows that the second term in (\ref{circle-01}) has no singularities in @@math:37:46b65a@@ This means that @@math:38:a3c64c@@ and @@math:39:486e1f@@ have the same singularities in @@math:40:b6275e@@

Now we are interested to know what happens on the circular boundary @@math:41:7d4acc@@ In this case we have that @@math:42:aa2c56@@ That is @@math:14:bd507f@@ Thus @@math:15:83fc59@@ which is entirely real. Therefore on the boundary @@math:43:4ca9bf@@ @@math:16:b9551b@@ This shows that the circle @@math:44:a78ad8@@ is a streamline.

Notice that the complex potential @@math:45:7b2ffb@@ satisfies the hypothesis of the Circle Theorem. We can therefore obtain the complex potential of the uniform flow around a circle by substituting @@math:46:339f4c@@ into equation (\ref{circle-theorem}): @@math:8:d927d1@@

Consequently, the stream function is just the imaginary part of (\ref{potential}), namely @@math:9:f12e0d@@ and we can see that the circle @@math:47:6b7e1a@@ is indeed a streamline, with @@math:48:15af3e@@ The resulting flow is shown in Figure 1 with @@math:49:abb691@@

You probably have noticed that (\ref{potential}) has a singularity at @@math:50:b59de5@@ This kind of singularity is known as a doublet and corresponds to the function @@math:51:e28985@@ The singularity at the origin is inside the obstacle and thus does not affect the external flow. The full streamline pattern, including the doublet inside the circle, is shown in Figure 2.

Uniform flow around circle with circulation

If we add a vortex to the complex potential defined in (\ref{potential}), we obtain a uniform flow around a circle with circulation: @@math:11:13f2c9@@ where @@math:55:0668a7@@ represents the circulation about the circle.

In this case, the streamfunction is

@@math:12:5bd8e3@@
Notice that the circle @@math:56:aa32d2@@ is still a streamline, with @@math:57:d92909@@ Any stagnation point in the flow satisfies the equation @@math:17:0fd1cb@@ which can be rearranged to the quadratic equation @@math:18:79c7ff@@ The roots of this equation are @@math:19:1335ea@@

Thus when @@math:58:eb00cb@@ there is no circulation with stagnation points at @@math:59:308e0a@@ As @@math:60:7fc634@@ increases, the anticlockwise circulation causes the stagnation points to move upwards around the circle. When it reaches the value 1, the two stagnation points coalesce at the top of the cylinder @@math:61:cdc717@@ If @@math:62:95aa5b@@ then one stagnation point moves into the flow; the other one is inside the circle.

Explore all the cases in the applet below which shows the flow and a circle of radius 1. Drag the sliders @@math:63:03086f@@ and @@math:64:aac86d@@ to change speed and circulation, respectively.
